did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Deep Learning and Generative AI to improve skin cancer diagnosis. Specifically, it employed Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs) via Stable Diffusion 1.5 with LoRA for data augmentation to address class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset. It also developed a novel classifier named SkinVit-Net, which is a hybrid architecture combining MobileNetV3 with Vision Transformer (ViT) attention heads, to perform skin lesion classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including the development of a novel hybrid architecture (SkinVit-Net), the use of Generative AI (Stable Diffusion with LoRA) for data augmentation, and comparative validation on the HAM10000 dataset. It reports specific quantitative results (e.g., 93.52% accuracy) and new findings, which classifies it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: Diffusion augmented SkinVit-Net to Improve Skin Cancer Diagnosis: Overcoming Data Imbalance to Support Robust Classification
journal: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of class imbalance in skin cancer diagnosis by proposing a novel hybrid architecture named SkinVit-Net, which combines MobileNetV3 with Vision Transformer attention heads. To mitigate data imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset, the authors employed Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs) via Stable Diffusion 1.5 with LoRA to generate realistic synthetic images for augmentation. The model's performance was evaluated against state-of-the-art baselines, including InceptionV3 and Xception, using three experimental designs: imbalanced, traditionally augmented, and diffusion-balanced datasets. SkinVit-Net trained on diffusion-balanced data achieved a classification accuracy of 93.52%, outperforming the other models and demonstrating superior generalization. The results indicate that diffusion-based augmentation combined with lightweight hybrid architectures effectively resolves class imbalance issues while maintaining low computational requirements suitable for clinical deployment.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset; noted as having the largest number of samples (majority class) causing imbalance.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (Nevus, Melanoma, Benign Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Vascular Lesion, Dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on robust classification of skin cancer using the SkinVit-Net model on the HAM10000 dataset. It reports classification accuracy (93.52%), precision, and F1 scores for seven discrete diagnostic categories. The study addresses class imbalance in this multi-class classification task.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, used as the primary dataset for this study to address class imbalance.

use.training: Used for training the SkinVit-Net classifier and the Stable Diffusion 1.5 model with LoRA for data augmentation. The dataset was split into training (70%), validation (15%), and testing (15%) sets with equal proportions of all classes.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training of the SkinVit-Net classifier and the diffusion model.
use.testing: Used for testing the performance of the SkinVit-Net classifier and baseline models (InceptionV3, Xception) across three experimental designs: imbalanced, traditionally augmented, and diffusion-balanced.
use.other: Used to generate synthetic images via Stable Diffusion 1.5 with LoRA to balance the dataset for underrepresented classes.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To report the uncertainty and precision of classification accuracy metrics for baseline models (InceptionV3, Xception) and the proposed SkinVit-Net across different dataset types (imbalanced, traditionally augmented, and diffusion-balanced).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported 95% Confidence Intervals for accuracy metrics to indicate uncertainty and precision, but did not perform statistical hypothesis tests to compare models.

imbalanced_dataset.metric: Accuracy (%), Precision (%), F1 Score (%) with 95% Confidence Intervals
imbalanced_dataset.notes: Baseline performance on original imbalanced data. SkinVit-Net outperformed baselines even without augmentation.

traditionally_augmented_dataset.metric: Accuracy (%), Precision (%), F1 Score (%) with 95% Confidence Intervals
traditionally_augmented_dataset.notes: Augmentation involved geometric and photometric techniques: rotation (20°), brightness [1.0, 1.3], shift (0.1), shear (0.1), zoom (0.1), and horizontal flips.

diffusion_balanced_dataset.metric: Accuracy (%), Precision (%), F1 Score (%) with 95% Confidence Intervals
diffusion_balanced_dataset.notes: Highest accuracy observed. SkinVit-Net achieved the best overall result. Diffusion augmentation provided greater improvements in classifier performance compared to traditional augmentation.

synthetic_image_quality.notes: Diffusion-generated images had slightly lower SSIM and PSNR but higher LPIPS, indicating perceptual similarity advantage and increased clinical heterogeneity which aids generalization.

architecture_used.pretraining: MobileNetV3-Small backbone (pretrained weights implied by standard usage, though not explicitly stated as ImageNet in the provided text, it is a standard backbone); Stable Diffusion 1.5 with LoRA for data augmentation (uses frozen CLIP ViT-L/14 text encoder).

architecture_details.hybrid_component: Vision Transformer (ViT)-inspired attention head appended to MobileNetV3
architecture_details.tokenization: Adaptive average pooling to 7x7 grid, resulting in 49 fixed tokens
architecture_details.attention_mechanism: Cross-attention blocks where latent queries attend to text embeddings (from CLIP)
architecture_details.aggregation_method: Mean pooling (averaging across all tokens) instead of adding a summary token

classification_or_output_head.type: Mean pooling aggregation followed by classification layer
classification_or_output_head.output_classes: 7 classes (Nevus, Melanoma, Benign Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Vascular Lesion, Dermatofibroma)
classification_or_output_head.details: Combines data by averaging across all 49 tokens from the transformer head; no extra summary token added.

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization according to standard medical image processing protocols
Geometric augmentation: Rotation (20°), Shift (0.1), Shear (0.1), Zoom (0.1), Horizontal flips
Synthetic data generation using Stable Diffusion 1.5 with LoRA for class balancing
Dataset splitting into training (70%), validation (15%), and testing (15%) sets with equal class proportions

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the traditionally augmented dataset. Settings: Rotation range 20 degrees, Brightness range [1.0, 1.3], Shift range 0.1, Zoom range 0.1, Shear intensity 0.1, and Horizontal flips.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Synthetic Data Generation via Diffusion Models
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used Stable Diffusion 1.5 with LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) for class balancing. The model used a frozen CLIP ViT-L/14 text encoder for conditioning. Training involved 3000 steps with checkpoints every 250 steps. Used to generate realistic and diverse images for underrepresented classes in the HAM10000 dataset.

conclusions: The proposed SkinVit-Net model, a hybrid architecture combining MobileNetV3 with Vision Transformer attention heads, achieved a classification accuracy of 93.52% on the HAM10000 dataset when trained on diffusion-balanced data, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines InceptionV3 (88.8%) and Xception (89.0%).

Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs) via Stable Diffusion 1.5 with LoRA effectively addressed class imbalance by generating realistic and diverse synthetic images for underrepresented skin lesion classes, leading to superior generalization compared to traditional geometric and photometric augmentation.

Diffusion-based augmentation provided greater improvements in classifier performance than traditional augmentation, with SkinVit-Net showing the most precise confidence intervals (92.0–95.0) and highest accuracy across all experimental designs.

Synthetic images generated by the diffusion model exhibited higher perceptual similarity (LPIPS: 0.5002) compared to traditionally augmented images (LPIPS: 0.4982), indicating that the morphological and textural diversity introduced by diffusion training aids in handling class imbalance and enhances downstream classification.

The lightweight nature of SkinVit-Net allows for deployment on mobile and resource-constrained devices without compromising diagnostic quality, offering a scalable solution for automated skin cancer diagnosis.

The study demonstrates that combining diffusion-based data augmentation with lightweight hybrid architectures can bridge the gaps between class imbalance and model complexity, providing a robust and deployable dermatology diagnostic tool.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single dataset (HAM10000) with no external validation on independent cohorts, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to compare model performance; it relies solely on 95% Confidence Intervals.

Synthetic image quality metrics (SSIM: 0.5524, PSNR: 12.82 dB) for diffusion-generated images were lower than those for traditionally augmented images (SSIM: 0.5703, PSNR: 14.29 dB), though the authors argue this reflects beneficial clinical heterogeneity.
The diffusion model training was conducted on a Google Colab T4 GPU with 16 GB GPU memory, which may not represent performance on higher-end clinical hardware or scalability for larger datasets.
The dataset split (70% train, 15% validation, 15% test) maintained equal proportions of all classes, which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence where class imbalance is inherent.

The paper claims the model is suitable for mobile and resource-constrained devices but does not provide inference latency, model size (parameters/weights), or memory footprint metrics for the final SkinVit-Net classifier.

Potential data leakage risk exists if the synthetic images generated by the diffusion model share latent features with the training set, although the paper does not explicitly address or mitigate this risk.
